LASER-BASED DIAGNOSTICS FOR CHARACTERIZING

James R. Gord,* Dale T. Shouse,f and W. M. Roquemore*
Propulsion Sciences and Advanced Concepts Division

Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
LASER-BASED DIAGNOSTICS FOR CHARACTERIZING
PRACTICAL COMBUSTOR HARDWARE
James R. Gord,* Dale T. Shouse,f and W. M. Roquemore*
Propulsion Sciences and Advanced Concepts Division
Air Force Research Laboratory
Wright-Patterson Air Force Base OH

title: Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
text:
Abstract
The Propulsion Directorate of the Air Force Research
Laboratory exists to provide propulsion and power
solutions essential to achieving and maintaining Global
Engagement—the Air Force Strategic Vision for the
21st Century. Factors driving the Directorate's mission
include the need to design, develop, test, and field high-
performance, low-emissions propulsion systems, for
both air and space, that are reliable, affordable,
maintainable, and supportable. 
Many of the goals
defining these efforts 
are communicated in the
Integrated 
High-Performance 
Turbine 
Engine
Technology (IHPTET) Program and the Integrated
High-Payoff Rocket Propulsion Technology (IHPRPT)
Program. The Combustion Branch of the Directorate's
Propulsion Sciences and Advanced Concepts Division
supports such efforts, particularly as they relate to gas
turbine 
combustion, through 
the activity 
of 
a
multidisciplinary team of scientists and engineers
representing government, industry, and academia. This
paper highlights recent Combustion-Branch efforts to
develop advanced laser-based diagnostic techniques
and apply these to fundamental studies in the
laboratory. 
Case studies showcase the transition of
diagnostic techniques from the laboratory to realistic
engine-test facilities in which practical combustor
hardware is characterized.

Abstract
The Propulsion Directorate of the Air Force Research
Laboratory exists to provide propulsion and power
solutions essential to achieving and maintaining Global
Engagement—the Air Force Strategic Vision for the
21st Century. Factors driving the Directorate's mission
include the need to design, develop, test, and field high-
performance, low-emissions propulsion systems, for
both air and space, that are reliable, affordable,
maintainable, and supportable. 
Many of the goals
defining these efforts 
are communicated in the
Integrated 
High-Performance 
Turbine 
Engine
Technology (IHPTET) Program and the Integrated
High-Payoff Rocket Propulsion Technology (IHPRPT)
Program. The Combustion Branch of the Directorate's
Propulsion Sciences and Advanced Concepts Division
supports such efforts, particularly as they relate to gas
turbine 
combustion, through 
the activity 
of 
a
multidisciplinary team of scientists and engineers
representing government, industry, and academia. This

develop advanced laser-based diagnostic techniques
and apply these to fundamental studies in the
laboratory. 
Case studies showcase the transition of
diagnostic techniques from the laboratory to realistic
engine-test facilities in which practical combustor
hardware is characterized.

text:
Introduction
Global Engagement—the Air Force Strategic Vision
for the 21st Century—demands enabling propulsion and
power solutions such as those currently under
development in the Propulsion Directorate of the Air
Force Research Laboratory. This strategic vision

requires high-performance systems designed to ensure
continued air and space superiority; however, signature-
reduction 
concerns 
and 
sensitivity 
to 
global
environmental 
issues 
demand 
low-emissions
performance of these propulsion systems as well. Often
the very conditions that yield high performance (such as
high-heat-release combustion) also contribute to the
formation of undesirable engine emissions (such as
nitric oxide, NO, and associated oxides of nitrogen,
NOX). 
Such challenges define the mission of the
Propulsion Directorate.
High-performance, affordability, and reliability goals
driving development of air-breathing gas turbine
engines 
are expressed 
in the Integrated High-
Performance Turbine Engine Technology (IHPTET)
Program. Established in 1988, this program teams the
Air Force, Army, Navy, DARPA, NASA, and industry
through 
cost-shared 
efforts 
designed 
to 
double
propulsion capability by around the turn of the century.
The three-phase IHPTET plan exploits advanced
materials development, innovative structural designs,
improved 
aerothermodynamics, 
and 
advanced
computational

title: Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
text:
methods 
to 
achieve 
well-defined
performance and cost goals. 
Because the roots of
nearly all commercial turbine engines can be traced to a
Department of Defense weapon-system-development
effort, IHPTET technology is inherently dual-use as
well.
Specific IHPTET activity is underway to address
engine components including turbomachinery (fans,
compressors, and turbines), combustors, mechanical
systems, exhaust systems, and controls. 
Supporting
efforts 
include 
the 
development 
of 
diagnostic
instrumentation, improved manufacturing techniques,
innovative analysis and design methodologies, and
specialized test facilities.
Similar programs describe other aspects of the
Propulsion-Directorate mission. 
For example, the
Integrated High-Payoff Rocket Propulsion Technology
(IHPRPT) Program mirrors IHPTET in many ways and
promises to double U.S. rocket propulsion capability.
The JP-8+100 Fuel Program exists to provide enabling
thermal-management solutions for current and future
1
American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics

Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
aircraft through the development of advanced thermally
stable fuels and fuel additives.
In 
addition 
to 
the 
high-performance 
drivers
represented by these programs, low-emissions goals
impact the continuing development of advanced
propulsion and power systems. 
The commercial
aviation industry is subject to restrictions on deleterious
engine emission such as NOX, carbon monoxide (CO),
unburned hydrocarbons (UHC's), volatile organic
compounds (VOC's), reactive organic gases (ROG's),
and particulate matter (PM). 
These regulations are
imposed at the state and federal level through the
Environmental 
Protection 
Agency 
(EPA) 
and
internationally through the International Civil Aviation
Organization (ICAO), for example. 
Maintaining the
dual-use characteristics of military propulsion solutions
demands Air Force sensitivity to these low-emissions
regulations.
While military aircraft and military bases are
typically exempt from such regulations, EPA rule
changes recently requested by the State of California
suggest reconsideration of the exempt status of military
aircraft. Furthermore, there exists a long-standing Air
Force policy (Attachment 3 to Air Force Regulation 19-
1) that states the Air Force will make a good-faith effort
to comply with EPA aircraft-emission regulations while
maintaining the high performance needed to ensure
flight safety and combat superiority. 
Air Force
signature-reduction 
goals 
associated 
with 
low-
observable-technology programs must be considered as
well during development of low-emissions gas turbine
engines.
All of these factors make up the framework in which
the Combustion Branch of the Directorate's Propulsion
Sciences and Advanced Concepts Division conducts its
day-to-day activity. High-performance, low-emissions
gas turbine combustion is pursued through efforts that
include laser-based 
diagnostics 
development 
and
application, simulation and modeling, fundamental
studies of aviation fuel and its combustion, and
associated engineering applications. Working hand-in-
hand with other Propulsion-Directorate elements,
particularly the Components Branch of the Turbine
Engine Division, Combustion-Branch scientists and
engineers seek to apply lessons learned during
fundamental studies to the design, development, and
testing of advanced combustor concepts consistent with
the IHPTET vision. 
Advanced propulsion concepts
extending beyond the IHPTET performance envelope
are explored as well.
The Combustion-Branch team includes Air-Force
scientists and engineers (both civil service and military
personnel), 
on- 
and 
off-site 
contractors, 
engine
manufacturers and subsystem/component suppliers, and
academicians who routinely participate in Branch
activity through sponsorship by the Air Force Office of
Scientific Research (AFOSR). 
Small businesses
responding to exploratory development needs through
the Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR)
Program also represent a vital component of this team.
Development 
and 
application 
of 
laser-based
diagnostic 
techniques 
are 
integrated 
throughout
Combustion-Branch programs. 
These techniques
represent powerful tools for non-invasive flowfield
visualization and quantification of fluid parameters and
key species concentrations. At a fundamental level,
diagnostics provide data essential to achieving a
detailed understanding of the chemistry and physics of
combusting flowfields. 
In addition, these data are
essential 
for 
validating combustion 
models 
and
combustor-design codes that promise to usher in a new
era in propulsion-system development. 
At a more
applied 
level, hardened 
diagnostics provide the
propulsion engineer with performance data critical to
the systems-engineering process. 
Diagnostics also
promise to play an important role in fielded propulsion
systems as elements in control and optimization
schemes.
These 
characteristics 
of 
laser-based 
diagnostic
techniques suggest a three-phase evolutionary process
for their development and application. In the initial
phase, emphasis is placed on the diagnostic technique
itself—on the chemistry and physics that define the
measurement and the hardware (sources, optics,
detectors, etc.) and software necessary to accomplish
that measurement. During this phase of the process, a
research-grade instrument is typically employed to
study a well-characterized flowfield in a laboratory
environment. 
While this phase of the diagnostics-
development process is certainly essential and exciting,
the ultimate utility of a diagnostic technique is
significantly limited if it is never applied beyond the
laboratory phase.
During the second phase of the process, a hardened
version of the research-grade instrument is applied to
achieving measurements in an engineering facility. The
emphasis in this phase shifts from the diagnostic
technique to the engineering application, such as gas
turbine engine development. This transition from the
laboratory to the facility is the subject of this session
and this paper. 
During the final phase of the
evolutionary process, a miniaturized 
and robust
diagnostic device might be incorporated into the final
product, such as an actual gas turbine engine, for on-
board sensing and control.
The transition from laboratory to facility is fraught
with significant challenges that must addressed. During
the laboratory phase of diagnostics development,
conditions are typically ideal and well-controlled. They
might involve vibrationally isolated laser tables,
humidity- and temperature-controlled environments,
and sufficient space to accommodate sources, optics,
American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics

Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
mounts, and detectors required to achieve the desired
measurement. Test articles often feature ample optical
access and support clean-burning, laminar flames
operating 
under 
atmospheric 
or 
sub-atmospheric
conditions. In contrast, facilities applications involve
conditions 
characteristic 
of 
actual 
gas 
turbine
combustor hardware. 
Challenges include extreme
environmental conditions (heat, vibration, acoustics,
etc.), 
fully 
developed turbulence, high pressure,
collisional quenching and energy redistribution, optical
thickness, beam steering, high background luminosity,
soot formation, limited optical access, tight geometric
constraints, limited operational space, and scattering
and spectral interferences. Successful transition from
the laboratory to the facility requires thoughtful
attention to these issues.
This paper continues with a brief description of
ongoing Combustion-Branch efforts to develop novel
diagnostic 
techniques 
and 
supporting 
hardware.
Facilities applications are highlighted through a
collection of case studies. Atmospheric-pressure and
high-pressure applications at Wright-Patterson Air
Force Base are presented

Facilities applications are highlighted through a

specialized facilities constructed to explore advanced
combustor concepts in cooperation with the Turbine

Facilities
construction and diagnostics applications underway
through Combustion-Branch SBIR programs with
Physical Sciences, Inc., and MetroLaser, Inc., complete
the paper.
Advanced Diagnostics Development
Developing 
advanced 
laser-based 
diagnostics
remains a mainstay of Combustion-Branch programs.
Current in-house and on-site-contract efforts have been
focused on exploiting the potential of high-repetition-
rate, 
modelocked 
laser 
sources 
for 
combustion
diagnostics applications. 
Continued development of
particle-image velocimetry (PIV) is underway as well.
In addition, SBIR programs are

title: Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
text:
Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
detailed 
parametric 
studies 
for 
evaluation 
and
calibration of LBO models.
The Task 200 includes a planar array of four injectors
configured to represent a simplified-geometry version
of an actual Pratt & Whitney annular combustor. The
airblast-atomizing fuel injectors employed are actual
engine hardware, and the major combustor dimensions
(dome height, length, and injector spacing) are based on
those associated with the engine in which those
injectors operate. The combustor liners and cooling-air
scheme were carefully configured to reproduce realistic
engine conditions. Pratt & Whitney design practice
was incorporated throughout the aerothermodynamic
design of the test section. Optical access is provided
through fused-quartz side walls and via the base of the
rig through a fused-quartz liner. A photograph of the
Task 200 combustor is depicted in Fig. 1, and a
schematic diagram is provided in Fig. 2.
Figure 1. Task 200 combustor.
: : :H2QCoolK(

Vi;,»Sx* tv+i
Figure 2. Schematic diagram of Task 200 combustor.
To accomplish LBO studies, the Task 200 combustor
was coupled to an air facility designed to provide up to
30 Ibm/s of air at ambient temperature and up to 23
Ibm/s of nonvitiated air at temperatures to 1200°F. Air
pressures to 750 psia are achievable in this facility;
however, the majority of the LBO study was conducted
at atmospheric pressure.
LBO studies with gaseous fuels were accomplished
with propane (available at 0.5 Ibm/min at 100 psig) and
methane (available at 0.4 Ibm/min at 150 psig), while
liquid-fuel studies were accomplished with ethanol
(available at 15 Ibm/hr at 200 psig). Bottled nitrogen
(available at 20 Ibm/min at 500 psig) was thoroughly
mixed with incoming air well upstream of the test
section during studies to simulate the effects of reduced
pressure on LBO characteristics.
Point measurements of velocity and temperature were
accomplished 
using 
a 
hardened

Recently, this effort has been extended to study the
impact of an advanced combustor geometry on the
operation of the CFM56 single-cup research combustor.
This modified configuration is depicted in Fig. 5.
Details of this particular study can not be released due
to its proprietary nature.
Room 151: Atmospheric-Pressure Facility
The combustion research facility housed in Building
490 Room 151 at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base
(Area B) was designed for the experimental exploration
of advanced combustor concepts under atmospheric-
pressure conditions with elevated inlet-air temperatures
Several different combustor test sections can be
installed in the facility, and each provides generous
optical access to the combustion flowfield. 
Test
sections are designed to contain single-cup or dual-cup
Figure 4. GE CFM56 swirl cup and Jet-A spray flame.

combustor-dome 
configurations. 
Three 
separate
metered, controlled, and heated air lines provide
combustion air to the test article. These three lines
supply dome or main air (20 Ibm/min at 700°F),
primary-zone air (7 Ibm/min at 700°F), and secondary-
zone or cooling air (7 Ibm/min at 700°F). 
A
photograph that includes the test section is depicted in
Fig 6.

and advanced geometry.
Figure 6.
facility.
Atmospheric-pressure combustion research
Exhaust System: The facility is connected to an
external exhaust-duct system via a rectangular duct
located at the exit of the combustor. This duct, which
routes exhaust gases vertically through the test-cell
ceiling, provides optical access to the combustor exit.
Two video cameras are available to document
combustor operation. One provides images from the
side of the test section, while the other provides images
at the rear of the test section through the exhaust duct.
Fuel System: 
The facility has two identical fuel
systems that supply liquid fuels (e.g., JP-8, ethanol) at
rates of 0.6 gpm and pressures to 300 psig. The two
systems are configured to accommodate fuel staging for
staged combustor systems operating in the facility.
Fuel flows are metered with turbine flowmeters and
controlled via remotely located valves. A gaseous fuel
system also exists to deliver propane or methane to the
test article.
Instrumentation: 
The facility utilizes PC-based
LabView software for data acquisition. The system is
configured with 64 K-type thermocouple channels for
the facility and test article, as well as Type B channels
for water-cooled thermocouple rakes. Incorporated are
32 pressure channels connected to a variety of psia,
psid, and psig transducers. A Kulite dynamic-pressure-
transducer system is employed to investigate combustor
instabilities through data collected on-line with a 16-
channel high-density multip

Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
to 15 atm. With stainless-steel blanks installed, the full
20-atm test-section capability is achievable.
The facility was designed to accommodate an optical
diagnostics system that encompasses the test section.
Diagnostics 
include 
CARS 
for 
temperature
measurements and planar laser-induced fluorescence
(PLIF) for flow visualization and quantification of key
combustion species. 
Encoded translations stages
controlling the transmitting and receiving optics permit
full three-dimensional spatial resolution of the test-
section flowfield.
Air-Supply System: 
Two Ingersoll-Rand air
compressors housed in the Test Air Facility located in
the lower level of the Building-18 complex furnish
combustion air to the Room-20 facility. Air mass-flow
rates to greater than 34 Ibm/s at pressures to 750 psig
are available. Air is routed through a natural-gas-fired,
non-vitiated heater that can deliver 34-lbm/s airflows to
the facility at temperatures to 1100°F. Flows to the
single-cup combustor system are maintained at 13
Ibm/s and 1100°F, while flows to the sector-combustor
system are maintained at 20 Ibm/s and 1100°F. In both
cases, combustor airflows are measured via a flow
venturi located in the inlet piping system.
Exhaust System: The facility is connected to four
Ingersoll-Rand exhausters that can achieve sub-
atmospheric pressures in the exhaust-piping header.
Exhaust gases are removed from the facility at a
sufficiently 
high rate to provide altitude-testing
capability. The facility can be configured to operate at
combustor-test-section pressures as low as 0.25 atm,
simulating altitud

has since been replaced with a high-pressure section
designed for operation to 50 atm that accommodates
actual engine hardware in the form of a General
Electric CFM56 swirl cup.

The facility described by Fig. 10 is designed to
deliver a maximum airflow rate of 1 kg/s at 530 K and
50 atm. The system operates in a blow-down mode,
providing ten-minute run times at CFM56 design
specifications with currently available storage capacity.
A separate low-pressure air-supply system is available
for facility preheating and purging. 
A fuel system
designed to supply the fuel injector with 0.55 gpm at
1500 psi completes the facility.

PSI gas turbine combustor simulator
A PLIF system based on a Nd:YAG-pumped dye
laser and an intensified charge-coupled device (CCD)
camera was recently constructed at PSI. This system
will be used in conjunction with the high-pressure,
heated air-supply system and the optically accessible
combustor to study OH, NO, PAH, fuel vapor, O2, and
soot 
volume 
fraction 
in 
combusting 
flowfields
generated with the CFM56 fuel injector. A large format
(1024x256 pixels) ICCD camera with a multi-viewer
imaging system was selected for this diagnostics
application. With this camera, images of target-species
fluorescence and interfering fluorescence can be
simultaneously 
acquired, 
reducing 
interferences
associated with single-shot PLIF imaging in these high-
pressure spray flames. Now that facility construction
and PLIF-system assembly have been completed,
combustion testing will soon commence.
Activity at MetroLaser, Inc.
Through a recently comp

Local temperature information can be extracted from
speed-of-sound measurements in the interaction volume
accomplished by monitoring the modulation frequency
of the time-resolved signal produced by the scattered
probe beam. A typical time-resolved signal and the
associated fit to obtain temperature/speed-of-sound
information are provided in Fig. 12.
Time (ns)
Figure 12. TGS signal (and associated fit) acquired in
the burnt gases of a rich ethylene/air flame.
By introducing a reference beam along the signal
path, heterodyne detection can be accomplished. Gas
velocities can be determined through analysis of
heterodyne-signal power spectra by measuring the
frequency shift of the heterodyne feature. 
Finally,
halfwidths of Rayleigh and Brillouin features in the
power spectrum provide pressure/density information.
A simulated heterodyne-mode power spectrum is
depicted in Fig. 13.
2
.£ 
0.8
10 
20 
30
Frequency (MHz)
Figure 13. TGS power spectrum with heterodyne
detection.
operating conditions. 
The signal amplitude grows
quadratically with pressure, and the technique does not
rely on the detection of 
electronically excited
molecules; therefore, TGS is enhanced with increasing
pressure 
and 
largely 
unaffected 
by 
collisional
quenching. 
Soot also enhances the TGS signal by
providing a means for coupling of the pump-induced
intensity grating to the surrounding medium via
thermalization. 
The spatially coherent signal beam
produced during TGS measurements is ideal for
working with test sections that offer limited optical
access. 
In addition, this signal beam is readily
s

title: Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
text:
Copyright© 1998, American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Inc.
critical feedback to the engineer seeking to evaluate and
improve the performance of actual engine hardware.
Finally, miniaturized devices based on these diagnostic
techniques can be incorporated into the actual fielded
engine for on-board sensing and control. Case studies
presented 
in 
this 
paper 
document 
continuing
development 
of 
advanced 
laser-based 
diagnostic
techniques and transition of such techniques to the
facility for practical application.
Acknowledgments
The authors gratefully acknowledge the continuing
efforts of all those associated with the Combustion-
Branch research and development team, particularly the
on-site work force, which includes Air Force personnel
and the talented employees of Innovative Scientific
Solutions, Inc., (Dr. Larry Goss, Principal Investigator)
and the University of Dayton Research Institute (Dr.
Dilip Ballal, Principal Investigator). Mr. Jeff Stutrud,
Mr. Chuck Frayne, and Dr. Robert Hancock of the
Combustion Branch have contributed immeasurably to
the on-site efforts documented in this paper. Drs. Mark
Alien and Mike Miller (Physical Sciences, Inc.) and Dr.
Mike Brown (MetroLaser, Inc.), who continue to
support the Air Force through first-class SBIR efforts,
provided the authors with a number of figures that
appear with their permission. The authors also thank
Dr. Fumi Takahashi who provided figures for this paper
and the associated presentation.
